front: What is the definition of a group in abstract algebra?
back: A group (G, *) is a set G closed under a binary operation * such that: 1) The operation is associative. 2) There exists an identity element e in G. 3) Every element a in G has an inverse a⁻¹ in G.

front: What is the difference between an abelian (commutative) group and a non-abelian group?
back: An abelian group is one where the group operation is commutative; that is, a * b = b * a for all a, b in G. If this property does not hold, the group is non-abelian.

front: What is the order of a group? What is the order of an element?
back: The order of a group |G| is the number of elements in the group. The order of an element g in G is the smallest positive integer n such that gⁿ = e (the identity). If no such n exists, the element has infinite order.

front: What is a subgroup? State the necessary and sufficient conditions for a subset H of a group G to be a subgroup.
back: A subgroup is a subset H of a group G that itself forms a group under the same operation. The one-step subgroup test states: A non-empty subset H of G is a subgroup if and only if for every a, b in H, the element a * b⁻¹ is also in H.

front: Define a cyclic group and provide the general notation for a cyclic group generated by an element a.
back: A cyclic group is a group that can be generated by a single element. This means every element in the group can be written as a power (or multiple, if using additive notation) of that generator. It is denoted ⟨a⟩ = { aⁿ | n is an integer }.
